Tomato___Early_blight.description: Early blight is a common fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani. It creates dark, concentric ring spots resembling a bull's-eye on older, lower leaves and can severely reduce yield if untreated.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (bull's-eye pattern)
Yellow halo surrounding the lesions
Lesions appear first on older, lower leaves
Stems may develop dark lesions near the soil line
Premature defoliation in severe cases
prevention: Practice crop rotation — avoid tomatoes in the same bed for 3–4 years
Use certified disease-free or resistant seed varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation; water at the base
Maintain proper plant spacing (60 cm) for good air circulation
Remove and destroy infected plant debris after harvest
Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the ground
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicide (Copper Oxychloride) every 7–10 days
Use chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicides at first sign of disease
Remove and bag infected leaves immediately — do not compost
Apply neem oil spray (5 ml/L) as an organic alternative
Spray potassium bicarbonate solution to suppress fungal growth

Tomato___Late_blight.description: Late blight is a destructive disease caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans — the same pathogen responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. It spreads rapidly in cool, wet conditions and can destroy an entire crop within days.
symptoms: Irregular, water-soaked green or grey lesions on leaves
White fuzzy mold on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Dark brown to black lesions on stems
Fruit develops firm, dark brown, greasy-looking rot
Rapid collapse and death of infected tissue
prevention: Plant certified disease-free transplants from reputable sources
Choose late-blight resistant varieties (e.g., Legend, Defiant)
Avoid working in wet fields to prevent spreading spores
Remove volunteer tomato and potato plants nearby
Ensure excellent drainage to reduce moisture around roots
Monitor weather — disease thrives at 10–25°C with high humidity
treatment: Apply systemic fungicides (metalaxyl, cymoxanil) at first outbreak
Use preventive copper fungicide sprays before disease appears
Immediately remove and burn (do not compost) all infected plants
Spray chlorothalonil or mancozeb as protective treatments
Reapply fungicides every 5–7 days during wet weather

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: Bacterial spot is caused by Xanthomonas species and thrives in warm, wet conditions. It affects leaves, stems, and fruit, causing significant cosmetic and yield losses.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked, greasy spots on leaves
Spots turn brown/black with a yellow halo
Raised, scab-like lesions on fruit surface
Premature defoliation leaving fruit exposed to sunscald
Dark streaks on stems and leaf petioles
prevention: Use certified pathogen-free seeds and resistant varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation and working with wet plants
Disinfect tools and equipment with 10% bleach solution
Practice 2–3 year crop rotation
Remove and destroy infected plant material promptly
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides weekly during wet weather
Use fixed copper plus mancozeb combination sprays
Remove heavily infected plants to prevent spread
Avoid nitrogen over-fertilisation, which promotes lush susceptible growth

Tomato___Leaf_Miner.description: Leaf miners are larvae of flies or moths (e.g., Liriomyza spp.) that tunnel inside leaf tissue, creating distinctive winding trails. Heavy infestations weaken the plant and reduce photosynthesis.
symptoms: Winding, pale serpentine trails (mines) visible on leaves
Tiny white or yellowish blotches on leaf surface
Infested leaves may yellow and drop prematurely
Small exit holes where adult insects emerge
prevention: Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce adult fly population
Install fine mesh netting over young transplants
Encourage natural predators (parasitic wasps, birds)
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertiliser that attracts egg-laying adults
Inspect transplants carefully before planting
treatment: Remove and destroy heavily mined leaves
Apply spinosad or abamectin insecticides — effective against larvae
Use neem oil spray as an organic deterrent
Introduce parasitic wasps (Diglyphus isaea) for biological control

Tomato___Mosaic_virus.description: Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV) is a highly contagious plant virus that stunts growth, distorts leaves, and reduces fruit quality and yield. It spreads through contact, infected seeds, and tools.
symptoms: Mottled light and dark green mosaic pattern on leaves
Leaf curling, puckering, and distortion
Stunted, slow plant growth
Yellowish-orange streaks on fruit
Internal fruit browning in severe cases
prevention: Use ToMV-resistant or tolerant tomato varieties
Start with certified virus-free seeds
Wash hands thoroughly before handling plants
Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach or 70% alcohol between plants
Control aphids and other insect vectors aggressively
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
treatment: No chemical cure exists — prevention is the only strategy
Remove and destroy infected plants to protect surrounding crops
Control vector insects (aphids, whiteflies) with insecticidal soap
Reduce plant-to-plant contact to slow spread

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: Septoria leaf spot is caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. It is one of the most common tomato diseases, starting on lower leaves and progressively moving upward, causing severe defoliation.
symptoms: Circular spots with dark brown borders and greyish-white centres
Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in the centre of spots
Lower leaves infected first, disease moves upward
Infected leaves turn yellow, then brown, then drop off
Severe defoliation exposes fruit to sunscald
prevention: Practice crop rotation (at least 3 years)
Avoid overhead watering; use drip irrigation
Stake or cage plants to improve air circulation
Remove and destroy infected leaves and crop debris
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper fungicide at first sign
Repeat sprays every 7–10 days during wet weather
Remove infected lower leaves to slow upward spread
Apply neem oil as an organic treatment option

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Two-spotted spider mites (Tetranychus urticae) are tiny arachnids that pierce leaf cells and suck plant sap. They thrive in hot, dry conditions and can cause severe damage very quickly.
symptoms: Fine webbing on undersides of leaves
Tiny yellow or white stippling (speckled) on leaf surface
Leaves turn bronze or reddish-brown with heavy infestation
Leaves dry out, curl, and drop prematurely
Visible tiny moving dots (mites) on leaf undersides
prevention: Maintain adequate soil moisture — mites prefer dry conditions
Use reflective mulch to deter mites
Encourage natural predators (predatory mites, lacewings)
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertiliser
Monitor plants regularly, especially in hot weather
treatment: Spray forcefully with water to knock mites off leaves
Apply insecticidal soap or neem oil to affected areas
Use miticides (abamectin, bifenazate) for severe infestations
Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiidae) for biocontrol
Remove and dispose of heavily infested plant material

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: Target spot is caused by the fungus Corynespora cassiicola. It produces distinctive concentric ring lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit and spreads rapidly in warm, humid environments.
symptoms: Brown circular lesions with dark concentric rings (target-like)
Yellow halo around lesions
Lesions on fruit appear as dark, sunken spots
Infected leaves yellow and fall prematurely
Stem lesions cause girdling and plant dieback
prevention: Plant resistant varieties where available
Ensure proper plant spacing for air flow
Avoid overhead irrigation
Remove and destroy infected plant material
Practice annual crop rotation
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or copper fungicides preventively
Use systemic fungicides (azoxystrobin) for curative treatment
Remove infected leaves at the first sign of disease
Apply neem oil as an organic alternative

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is a devastating viral disease transmitted by the silverleaf whitefly. Infected plants are severely stunted and rarely produce marketable fruit.
symptoms: Upward curling and cupping of leaves
Yellowing (chlorosis) of leaf margins and interveinal areas
Leaves become small, leathery, and deformed
Severe stunting of plant growth
Significant flower drop and poor fruit set
prevention: Plant TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties (most important strategy)
Use reflective silver mulch to deter whiteflies
Install fine insect-proof netting in seedling nurseries
Eliminate weeds that serve as virus reservoirs
Monitor whitefly populations with yellow sticky traps
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
treatment: No cure exists — remove infected plants immediately to limit spread
Control whitefly vector with insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam)
Use insecticidal soap or neem oil for organic whitefly control
Apply reflective mulch to reduce whitefly landing on plants

Tomato___healthy.description: Your tomato plant appears healthy! No signs of disease have been detected. Continue with good cultural practices to keep your plants productive throughout the season.
symptoms: Deep green, firm leaves with no spots or discolouration
Strong, upright stems
Normal leaf size and shape
Vigorous growth and flowering
prevention: Water consistently at the base — avoid wetting foliage
Fertilise with balanced NPK every 2–3 weeks
Stake or cage plants for proper support
Inspect plants weekly for any early signs of pests or disease
Rotate crops each season to prevent soil-borne diseases
Mulch around plants to retain moisture and suppress weeds
treatment: No treatment needed — maintain current healthy care routine
Continue monitoring for early pest or disease signs
Ensure adequate sunlight (6–8 hours daily)
Prune suckers to improve air circulation and fruit size

Potato___Early_blight.description: Early blight of potato is caused by Alternaria solani. It primarily affects older leaves with characteristic dark concentric-ring lesions, and can significantly reduce tuber yield if left unmanaged.
symptoms: Dark brown circular spots with concentric rings (bull's-eye pattern)
Yellow halo surrounding lesions
Lower, older leaves are infected first
Lesions may coalesce causing large dead patches
Premature leaf drop exposing tubers to sunscald
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Practice 3-year crop rotation avoiding Solanaceae family
Plant resistant varieties where available
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip or furrow watering
Maintain adequate nitrogen levels to reduce plant stress
Remove and destroy crop debris after harvest
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide at first sign
Use copper-based fungicides as an organic option
Reapply every 7–10 days during warm humid weather
Remove and bag heavily infected leaves — do not compost
Apply neem oil spray as a preventive organic treatment

Potato___Late_blight.description: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) is the most devastating potato disease — the pathogen responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. It spreads explosively in cool, wet conditions and can destroy an entire crop within days.
symptoms: Irregular water-soaked lesions on leaves that quickly turn brown-black
White fuzzy mold on underside of leaves in humid conditions
Dark brown stem lesions causing plant collapse
Tubers develop reddish-brown rot beneath the skin
Rapid spread — entire field can collapse within a week
prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed tubers only
Choose late-blight resistant potato varieties
Hill up soil around stems to protect tubers from spore runoff
Avoid overhead irrigation and working in wet fields
Remove volunteer potato plants and solanaceous weeds
Monitor weather — high risk in cool (10–25°C), wet periods
treatment: Apply systemic fungicides (metalaxyl, cymoxanil) immediately at first sign
Use preventive copper fungicide sprays before disease appears
Remove and destroy (burn) all infected material — never compost
Harvest tubers as soon as possible if disease is widespread
Re-apply fungicides every 5–7 days during wet weather

Potato___healthy.description: Your potato plant appears healthy! No signs of disease detected. Maintain good cultural practices to maximise tuber yield throughout the season.
symptoms: Deep green, firm leaves with no spots or discolouration
Strong stems with vigorous growth
Normal leaf size and shape without curling or distortion
Good canopy cover indicating healthy photosynthesis
prevention: Water consistently at the base — avoid wetting foliage
Hill soil around stems to protect developing tubers
Fertilise with balanced NPK plus adequate potassium
Scout weekly for early signs of blight, aphids, or beetles
Practice annual crop rotation to prevent soil-borne diseases
Harvest when foliage naturally dies back for best storage quality
treatment: No treatment needed — maintain current healthy care routine
Continue monitoring for early pest or disease signs
Ensure adequate hilling to prevent tuber greening
Control Colorado potato beetle to protect foliage

Tomato__Target_Spot.description: Target spot is caused by the fungus Corynespora cassiicola. It produces distinctive concentric ring lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit, spreading rapidly in warm, humid conditions.
symptoms: Brown circular lesions with dark concentric rings (target-like appearance)
Yellow halo surrounding lesions
Lesions on fruit appear as dark, sunken spots
Infected leaves yellow and fall prematurely
Stem lesions can cause girdling and plant dieback
prevention: Plant resistant varieties where available
Ensure proper plant spacing for good air circulation
Avoid overhead irrigation
Remove and destroy infected plant material promptly
Practice annual crop rotation
treatment: Apply chlorothalonil or copper fungicide preventively
Use systemic fungicides (azoxystrobin) for curative treatment
Remove infected leaves at first sign of disease
Apply neem oil as an organic alternative

_default.description: This disease class was not found in the reference database. The model has identified a potential issue — consult a local agricultural extension service for expert diagnosis.
symptoms: Refer to a plant pathologist for accurate symptom analysis
prevention: Maintain good general plant hygiene and crop rotation
treatment: Consult a local agricultural expert or extension service
